You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — method-vol-1-chord-construction

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc

The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 is structured as a strict bottom-up curriculum that moves from notation literacy to a working theory of diatonic harmony on the guitar. Chapter 1 establishes the notation vocabulary: the staff, the treble/bass/C clef family (with the treble G-clef privileged for guitar), and the grand staff. Chapter 2 builds the pitch and interval system on top of that notation - the diatonic and chromatic scales, the W-W-H-W-W-W-H major formula, the relative minor as the 6th-degree rotation, and a full interval taxonomy (melodic/harmonic, simple/compound, diatonic/chromatic, enharmonic). Chapter 3 turns those scales into a system of key relationships via the Circle of Fourths and Fifths, with explicit memorization anchors (orders of sharps and flats) and mirror-image identification rules. Chapter 4 - the densest chapter - constructs the chord universe by tertian stacking. Chapter 5 organizes those chords into harmonic fields and the seven modes, Chapter 6 prescribes how to practice those harmonic fields, and Chapter 7 extends the field idea to Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major as color sources rather than progression engines. The pedagogical sequence is itself a thesis: notation, then scales, then key relationships, then chords, then fields, then practice method, then chromatic color.

## The Fretboard-as-Matrix System

The book's load-bearing system is a chord-first visualization of the fretboard. Chapter 4 declares the fretboard a matrix to be navigated chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases derived from chord knowledge rather than the reverse. Chord shapes are organized into four root-string zones - 6th, 5th, 4th, and (for triads only) 3rd string - and Chapter 2 supplies the spatial scaffolding for this: the Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the fretboard repeats at the 12th fret, and two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. Chapter 2 also issues the methodological rule that shapes and intervals must be learned together, never separately. Chapters 6 and 7 then restate a universal practice rule across every harmonic field and every derived family: locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings first, and defer 4th-string voicings until those are mastered.

## The Chord-Family System (Chapter 4)

Chapter 4 partitions the chord universe into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). The signature device is the Chord Skeleton: pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants. Triads have four qualities and three inversions (root, first, second), with the rule that diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric. Tetrads expand to Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework; an unmarked 7 is minor by convention (major 7 must be explicitly indicated); Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations are restricted to tetrads; and enharmonic identities are formalized (C6 = Am7 first inversion; Cm(b6) = Abmaj7 first inversion). Extended Chords (9, 11, 13) codify the #11-versus-natural-11 rule (use #11 on major and dominant chords to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely), define altered chords as dominants with more than one altered extension, and note that the 5th is almost always omitted in 13ths. Voice leading is governed by a smallest-movement rule, and a specific voicing prohibition forbids a flat-9 between bass and top note on major or minor chords. Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2, sus4) and are taught by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes. A load-bearing instrument constraint runs throughout: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes, and this drives nearly every voicing choice.

## The Harmonic Field System (Chapters 5-6)

The harmonic field is the book's organizing frame for harmony in practice. Chapter 5 builds it by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords in Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm), and pairs each degree with one of the seven modes (Ionian through Locrian) as rotations of the parent scale. The book's central rule here is that modes drive both harmony and melody by revealing each chord's tensions and the correct scale choice. Chapter 5 then introduces a four-category taxonomy of scale notes that becomes load-bearing for the rest of the book: Chord Notes (1-3-5(-7)), Extended Notes (9, 11, 13 tensions), Avoid Notes (usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones), and Characteristic Notes (those that give the mode its identity). It distinguishes the Natural Major and Natural Minor Harmonic Fields as the 'happy' and 'sad' frames sharing chords but differing in analytic vantage, and clarifies that listed extensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components. Chapter 6 then prescribes how to study these fields: a four-rule Study Devices protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string), the Visualization via bass note principle, and a degree-by-degree substitution catalog (e.g., the V dominant-to-sus substitution G7 -> Gsus9/Gsus13, the IIIm phrygian Em7(b6) and slash voicings, the VIIm locrian recast of Bo as G7(9)/B with the 9th forbidden because it produces a b9 avoid note).

## Color Scales and the Leading-Tone Argument (Chapter 7)

Chapter 7 grounds the rest of the book's chromatic vocabulary in a single functional definition: the leading tone is the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale, the half-step tension the Natural Minor lacks. This historical absence motivated raising the natural minor's VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, then the Melodic Minor Scale (treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form rather than the older ascending-only convention), and finally Rimsky-Korsakov's Harmonic Major Scale. Each parent scale generates seven scale modes - the book deliberately calls these scale modes rather than harmonic fields, because the rule is that their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices layered over existing chords. Signature derived modes are spelled out: from Melodic Minor, the VII Altered Scale, bIII Lydian Augmented, and IV Lydian Dominant; from Harmonic Major, bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/#5) and VIIm Locrian (bb7), the latter sourcing o7(b13) and (11) colors. A recurring VERY IMPORTANT rule restates that extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the volume teaches an integrated method with four interlocking commitments. First, terminology is fixed and load-bearing: harmonic field, diatonic chords, Degrees, the seven mode names, the Chord/Extended/Avoid/Characteristic note categories, Chord Skeletons, Guide Notes, Drop 2/Drop 3, leading tone, scale modes, Bach Scale. Second, the fretboard is taught chord-first with bass-note visualization on the 5th and 6th strings, 4th-string voicings deferred, and shapes-with-intervals learned as a single unit. Third, practice is rule-governed: one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before modulation, and proximity-based chord selection. Fourth, the analytic frame separates structural diatonic harmony (the natural major and minor harmonic fields of Chapters 5-6) from chromatic color (the derived scale-mode families of Chapter 7), with explicit rules - the smallest-movement voice-leading rule, the #11-versus-11 rule, the no-flat-9-between-bass-and-top rule, the sus-as-3rd-replacement rule, the no-inversions-for-symmetric-chords rule, the VII-9 forbidden in Locrian, and the omit-the-5th-in-13ths convention - constraining every voicing choice. The instrument constraint that the guitar must edit and omit notes, stated in Chapter 4, sits behind the whole method and explains why visualization, families, and bass-note anchoring carry the pedagogical weight that piano-centric theory would assign to literal voice-leading.

# Chapter 1 — Staff, Clefs & Musical Notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 is foundational and introductory rather than guitar-technique instruction, laying out the notation vocabulary the rest of the book depends on. It defines the musical interval and its half-step/whole-step units (Interval definition), then establishes that Western music uses 12 pitches built from 7 natural notes mapped from solfege to letters (Do=C through Ti=B) with accidentals supplying the remaining five (Natural notes and letters, Accidentals derive remaining notes). It introduces the staff as five lines and four spaces and explains the clef as the symbol that anchors note names to those positions (Staff definition, Clef purpose), then walks through the Treble/G clef on line 2 used for guitar, the Bass/F clef on line 4, and the C clef on line 3, with a reference table of line/space notes for each (Treble (G) clef, Bass (F) clef, C clef, G clef line/space notes). It enumerates the ten clef-position variants while noting that popular-music guitarists work mainly with treble and bass (Clef subdivisions and positions, Popular-music clef usage), and closes by defining the grand staff and the term system used in piano/harp scores (Grand Staff). No master's stylistic moves appear here; the chapter is pure notation literacy.

# Chapter 2 — Musical Scales & Intervals

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 lays the theoretical and notational groundwork the rest of the book depends on, beginning with the diatonic scale built on the seven natural notes with half steps between B-C and E-F, the major-scale formula W-W-H-W-W-W-H, the natural (relative) minor taken from the 6th degree with its W-H-W-W-H-T-T pattern, and the 12-note chromatic universe. It then codifies the book's diagram conventions: left-hand finger numbering 1-4, the X (muted) and O (open) symbols, fret-number indicators tied to the nearest finger, arched-line barres with repeated-number notation for non-index barres, and the rule that scale diagrams show intervals over a chosen root rather than fingerings. A spatial framework is introduced in which the Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the fretboard repeats at the 12th fret, and notes are memorized in steps using open strings, naturals on the 6th and 5th strings, and octave shortcuts to the 4th, 3rd, and 2nd strings (with the B-string anomaly handled via a 4th-string octave). A core methodological rule is stated explicitly under the Shapes vs intervals warning - shapes and intervals must be learned together, anchored by two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings that serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. The chapter then defines the load-bearing interval vocabulary: melodic vs harmonic, simple vs compound, diatonic vs chromatic, and enharmonic intervals, preparing the reader for later chord-spelling and alteration work. Ear training is prescribed via the song-reference method, with complete ascending and descending interval tables (24 songs total, from Isn't She Lovely through Willow Weep for Me) and a full inversion table mapping each ascending interval to its descending complement, foundational for chord inversions and voice leading.

# Chapter 3 — Circle of Fourth / Circle of Fifth

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the Circle of Fourths and Circle of Fifths as the system of relationships among major and minor keys on the Western chromatic scale, anchored in the C Natural Major Scale and its W-W-H-W-W-W-H interval pattern. Under "Preserving major-scale sound," it frames sharps and flats not as arbitrary accidentals but as modifications required to preserve that pattern in other keys, and then enumerates the canonical sequences: C, G, D, A, E, B, F#, C# for the sharp (fifth) circle and C, F, Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Cb for the flat (fourth) circle. The chapter drills the order of sharps (F, C, G, D, A, E, B) and flats (B, E, A, D, G, C, F) as a memorization anchor, then gives two mirror-image identification rules with worked examples: the last sharp is a half step below the major key (three sharps ending on G# yields A major), while for flats the one before the last is the key (three flats ending on Ab gives Eb major). It also covers notational mechanics under "Key signature placement" and "Canceling a key change" — the signature sits between clef and time signature, and modulations are written by naturalizing the old accidentals before introducing the new signature. Finally, under "Major vs minor and relative key," it introduces the load-bearing term relative key, defined as the VI degree of the major and characterized as the "sad" minor counterpart to each major signature.

# Chapter 4 — Chord Structures

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 builds the book's central thesis that the fretboard is a matrix to be visualized chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases flowing out of chord knowledge organized into four root-string zones (6th, 5th, 4th, and 3rd strings, the last only for triads). The chord universe is partitioned into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony, the superimposition of thirds (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). Under Triads, the chapter gives interval formulas for the four qualities, names the three inversion positions (root, first, second), introduces slash-chord notation (e.g., C/E), and presents the book's signature 'Chord Skeletons' - pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants, plus playability caveats (e.g., avoid the 6th-string left-side minor shape) and the rule that diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric. Tetrad Chords expand to Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework, the convention that an unmarked 7 is minor (major 7 must be indicated), enharmonic equivalences (C6 = Am7/first-inversion; Cm(b6) = Abmaj7/first-inversion), and Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations restricted to tetrads. Voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule, and characteristic voicing prohibitions appear - notably avoiding a flat-9 between bass and top note on major/minor chords. Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4), and the chapter recommends visualizing them by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes, decoding jazz symbols like Gsus9 and Gsus13 into precise tone content. Extended Chords cover 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions, codifying the #11-vs-natural-11 rule (use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely), defining altered chords as dominants with more than one altered extension, and noting that the 5th is almost always omitted in 13th chords. The chapter also maps 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes (typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord) and surfaces the dominant-7 web of enharmonic relationships with diminished, half-diminished, and maj7(#4) chords. A load-bearing instrument constraint runs throughout: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes, which drives nearly every voicing choice in the chapter.

# Chapter 5 — Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches how the harmonic field is built by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords labeled with upper/lowercase Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm), with accidentals like bII, bIII, #IV available when needed. It defines the 7 Modes as rotations of the natural major scale (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian), giving the exact note sets in C and declaring the book's central rule that modes drive both harmony and melody by revealing each chord's tensions and correct scale choice. The chapter introduces a four-category taxonomy of scale notes -- Chord Notes (triads/tetrads 1-3-5(-7)), Extended Notes (9, 11, 13 tensions), Avoid Notes (usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones), and Characteristic Notes (those that give the mode its identity) -- and provides reference cards for Ionian, Lydian (with its #11), Mixolydian (dominant-7th profile with avoid 4th), and Locrian (half-diminished with avoid b2). It distinguishes the Natural Major and Natural Minor Harmonic Fields, framing them as the 'happy' vs. 'sad' organizing systems that share the same chords but differ in analytic frame, and tabulates the natural minor degrees Im, IIm(b5), bIII, IVm, Vm, bVI, bVII. A clarifying rule notes that listed extended notes are melodic passing-tone options, not mandatory voicing components. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes harmonic field, diatonic chords, Degrees, the seven mode names, and the Chord/Extended/Avoid/Characteristic note categories.

# Chapter 6 — How to Study the Harmonic Fields

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 lays out a practice methodology and substitution catalog for studying the diatonic harmonic fields on guitar, opening with a four-rule Study Devices protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, and 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string ones) and a Visualization via bass note principle that anchors chord study to roots on the 5th and 6th strings rather than to scale shapes. Each diatonic degree is then walked through with its modal character and allowable swaps: the I degree's C6/Cmaj7 interchange with a b9-conflict exception when the melody is the octave, the IIm dorian extensions Dm7(9)/(11)/(13) with m6 deferred, the IIIm phrygian additions like Em7(b6), Cmaj7/E, C/E, Esus(b9) and the Jobim/Villa-Lobos Em7(b9), the IV lydian's optional #4 with G/F, G7(13), G7(9) implying F lydian, the V dominant-to-sus substitution (G7 to Gsus9/Gsus13, Dm/G as mixolydian), and the VIm aeolian extensions Am7(9)/(11)/(b6) plus Dm7/A and Fmaj7/A slash voicings. The chapter closes on the VIIm locrian avoid-note rule, recasting Bo as G7(9)/B and forbidding the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note. The characteristic move throughout is reinterpreting each chord as a slash-chord or upper-structure of a neighboring diatonic chord while flagging which characteristic tones are optional. Load-bearing terminology introduced or formalized here includes harmonic fields, bass-note visualization, characteristic notes, avoid notes, sus substitution, and the modal labels (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian) tied to each diatonic degree.

# Chapter 7 — Leading Tone & How Other Scales Appeared

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 builds its entire framework on the functional definition of the leading tone as the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale, the half-step tension that the Natural Minor lacked and that historically motivated raising its VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, followed by the Melodic Minor Scale (originally ascending-only, now treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form) and Rimsky-Korsakov's mid-twentieth-century Harmonic Major Scale. These three new families each generate seven scale modes (deliberately called scale modes rather than harmonic fields), and the chapter is emphatic that their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices that add tension on top of existing chords. Load-bearing interval spellings are given for each parent mode (Im Harmonic Minor with min 3rd/min 6th/maj 7th, Im Melodic Minor with min 3rd/maj 6th/maj 7th, I Harmonic Major as major scale with b6) and for the signature derived modes the families produce: the VII Altered Scale, bIII Lydian Augmented, and IV Lydian Dominant from Melodic Minor, plus Harmonic Major's bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/#5) and VIIm Locrian (bb7), the latter sourcing o7(b13) and (11) colors. Each mode is specified by avoid notes and characteristic notes (e.g., Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th), and a recurring VERY IMPORTANT rule clarifies that extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord. The master's characteristic moves are pedagogical: a universal fretboard-visualization rule restated for each family (locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings only, with 4th-string chords deferred until comfort), plus the study devices of practicing harmonic fields in the same key, always with a rhythmic feel (funk, swing), and choosing chords close to one another for proximity. The terminology this chapter makes load-bearing is leading tone, scale modes, derived chord families as color, avoid/characteristic notes, Bach Scale, Altered, Lydian Augmented, Lydian Dominant, and Locrian bb7.

    {
      "id": "benson:method-vol-1:chord-families",
      "name": "Chord-Family System (Triads, Tetrads, Extended)",
      "summary": "Chapter 4 partitions the chord universe into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). The signature device is the Chord Skeleton: pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants. Voicings are continually shaped by a load-bearing instrument constraint: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "triads",
          "name": "Triads",
          "summary": "Four qualities (major, minor, diminished, augmented); three inversion positions (root, first, second)."
        },
        {
          "id": "tetrads",
          "name": "Tetrads",
          "summary": "Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework."
        },
        {
          "id": "extended",
          "name": "Extended Chords",
          "summary": "9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions."
        },
        {
          "id": "suspended",
          "name": "Suspended Chords",
          "summary": "sus2/sus4 defined strictly as 3rd-replacement, visualized by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes."
        },
        {
          "id": "chord-skeletons",
          "name": "Chord Skeletons",
          "summary": "Pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "smallest-movement-voice-leading",
          "name": "Smallest-Movement Voice Leading",
          "summary": "Between adjacent chords, prefer the voicing that moves each voice the smallest distance.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "VoiceMotion"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Voice leading",
              "quote_excerpt": "voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sus-anchored-to-dominant-7",
          "name": "Sus Chords Anchored to Dominant-7 Shapes",
          "summary": "Move into a suspended voicing by replacing the 3rd within a known dominant-7 shape.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Suspended chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "visualizing them by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "4th-string-from-6th-5th",
          "name": "4th-String Extended Voicings Derived From 6th/5th",
          "summary": "Map 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes, typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "StringSetTransition"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "4th-string extended voicings",
              "quote_excerpt": "derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes"
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "drop2-drop3-tetrads-only",
          "name": "Drop 2 / Drop 3 Restricted to Tetrads",
          "summary": "Drop 2 and Drop 3 voicing transformations are restricted to tetrads.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Tetrad Chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations restricted to tetrads"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "no-inversions-symmetric",
          "name": "No Inversions for Symmetric Chords",
          "summary": "Diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [

              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Triads",
              "quote_excerpt": "diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "omit-5th-in-13ths",
          "name": "Omit the 5th in 13th Chords",

              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Extended Chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "the 5th is almost always omitted in 13th chords"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "no-b9-above-bass",
          "name": "No b9 Between Bass and Top on Major/Minor",
          "summary": "On major and minor chords, avoid a flat-9 interval between the bass note and the top note of the voicing.",
          "engine_payload": {

          "references": [

          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sharp11-vs-natural11",
          "name": "#11 vs Natural 11 Rule",
          "summary": "Use #11 on major and dominant 7 chords to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Extended Chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sus-is-3rd-replacement",
          "name": "Sus Is 3rd-Replacement Only",
          "summary": "Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2 or sus4), never as additions.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Suspended chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4)"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "guitar-must-edit-and-omit",
          "name": "Guitar Must Edit and Omit Notes",
          "summary": "Unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes; this constraint drives nearly every voicing choice.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Instrument constraint",
              "quote_excerpt": "the guitar must edit and omit notes"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "unmarked-7-is-minor",
          "name": "Unmarked 7 Is Minor (Maj7 Must Be Marked)",
          "summary": "By notation convention, an unmarked 7 means minor 7; a major 7th must be explicitly indicated.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:notation-default"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Tetrad Chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "an unmarked 7 is minor (major 7 must be indicated)"
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "benson:method-vol-1:harmonic-fields",
      "name": "Harmonic Field System (Diatonic Modes and Substitutions)",
      "summary": "Chapter 5 builds the harmonic field by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords in Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm) and pairing each degree with one of the seven modes (Ionian through Locrian). Scale notes are classified as Chord Notes, Extended Notes, Avoid Notes, and Characteristic Notes. Chapter 6 then prescribes a degree-by-degree substitution catalog grounded in modal character; listed extensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "i-ionian",
          "name": "I (Ionian)"
        },
        {
          "id": "iim-dorian",
          "name": "IIm (Dorian)"
        },

          "id": "iiim-phrygian",
          "name": "IIIm (Phrygian)"
        },
        {
          "id": "iv-lydian",
          "name": "IV (Lydian)"
        },

        },

          "name": "VIm (Aeolian)"
        },
        {
          "id": "viim-locrian",
          "name": "VIIm (Locrian)"
        },
        {
          "id": "natural-major-field",
          "name": "Natural Major Harmonic Field"
        },

        }
      ],

          "references": [

        },

          "engine_payload": {

          "references": [

      "modification_rules": [

    {
      "id": "benson:method-vol-1:chromatic-color-scales",
      "name": "Chromatic Color-Scale System (Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, Harmonic Major)",
      "summary": "Chapter 7 builds its framework on the functional definition of the leading tone as the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale - the half-step tension the Natural Minor lacks. This absence motivated raising the natural minor's VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, then the Melodic Minor Scale (treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form), and finally Rimsky-Korsakov's Harmonic Major Scale. These parent families each generate seven scale modes - deliberately called scale modes rather than harmonic fields - because their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices layered over existing chords.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "harmonic-minor",
          "name": "Harmonic Minor Scale",
          "summary": "Im with min 3rd / min 6th / maj 7th; restores the leading tone to the natural minor."
        },
        {
          "id": "melodic-minor-bach",
          "name": "Melodic Minor Scale (Bach Scale form)",
          "summary": "Im with min 3rd / maj 6th / maj 7th, treated as one symmetric form."
        },
        {
          "id": "harmonic-major",
          "name": "Harmonic Major Scale",
          "summary": "Rimsky-Korsakov's mid-20th-century scale: major scale with b6."
        },
        {
          "id": "altered-scale",
          "name": "VII Altered Scale (from Melodic Minor)"
        },
        {
          "id": "lydian-augmented",
          "name": "bIII Lydian Augmented (from Melodic Minor)"
        },
        {
          "id": "lydian-dominant",
          "name": "IV Lydian Dominant (from Melodic Minor)"
        },
        {
          "id": "hm-lydian-augmented",
          "name": "bVI Lydian Augmented #2/#5 (from Harmonic Major)"
        },
        {
          "id": "locrian-bb7",
          "name": "VIIm Locrian bb7 (from Harmonic Major)",
          "summary": "Sources o7(b13) and (11) colors."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "color-layered-over-existing",
          "name": "Color Scales Layer Over Existing Chords",
          "summary": "Derived chord families are not progression-guiding; introduce them by layering coloristic tension over an existing diatonic chord, not by substituting the chord's function.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "Derived chord families as color",
              "quote_excerpt": "coloristic devices that add tension on top of existing chords"
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "leading-tone-as-color-source",
          "name": "Leading-Tone Restoration Generates Color Scales",
          "summary": "Raising the natural minor's VII restores the leading tone and motivates the Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major families as color sources.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "Leading tone definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "raising its VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "mode-avoid-and-characteristic",
          "name": "Each Derived Mode Specified by Avoid and Characteristic Notes",
          "summary": "Each derived scale mode is specified by its avoid notes and characteristic notes (e.g., Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th).",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "Avoid notes and characteristic notes",
              "quote_excerpt": "Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "extended-as-melodic-passing",
          "name": "Extended Notes as Passing Tones, Not Voicing Requirements",
          "summary": "VERY IMPORTANT rule: extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "VERY IMPORTANT rule",
              "quote_excerpt": "many belong only as passing tones in the melody"
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
